solver_view.stem: A battery safety engineer compares four cell groups: fresh cells, cells cycled at 25°C, cells cycled at -5°C, and cells from a used consumer device (dynamic cycling with calendar aging), all at similar SOH (~80%). During thermal abuse testing, voltage drops occur at relatively lower temperatures only in the consumer-device cells. Which conclusions are supported by this observation?
options: A Consumer-device cells are more sensitive to internal short circuit triggers than the other aged groups.
B The earlier voltage drop in consumer-device cells implies they also have the lowest self-heating onset temperature among all groups.
C Dynamic cycling combined with calendar aging produces a distinct aging signature not captured by constant-temperature lab cycling alone.
D The -5°C-cycled cells will show voltage drops at higher temperatures than the consumer-device cells because lithium plating raises internal resistance.
E Voltage drop temperature alone is sufficient to rank all four groups by thermal runaway severity.
F The consumer-device result demonstrates that real-world aging profiles can produce thermal behavior not predicted by single-variable lab aging protocols.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states consumer-device cells are more sensitive to internal short circuit triggers based on early voltage drop.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence identifies lowest self-heating onset in -5°C-cycled cells; no evidence links consumer-device cells to lowest onset temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Consumer-device aging combines dynamic cycling and calendar aging, producing distinct voltage-drop behavior not seen in isothermal lab cycling.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports ranking -5°C cells above consumer-device cells on voltage-drop temperature; this conflates resistance increase with short-circuit sensitivity.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not support voltage drop temperature as a sufficient universal ranking metric for TR severity across all conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Consumer-device result shows real-world multi-variable aging produces behavior distinct from controlled single-variable lab protocols.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing internal short circuit sensitivity from self-heating onset; evaluating scope of lab aging protocols
analysis.primary_boundary: Consumer-device finding is specific to dynamic+calendar aging; cannot be generalized to all real-world cells
intended_error_modes: mechanism_substitution between short-circuit sensitivity and self-heating onset
regime_transfer_error conflating resistance with short-circuit trigger
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming consumer-device cells have lowest TR onset among all groups
Using voltage drop temperature as universal TR severity metric

solver_view.stem: A pouch lithium-ion battery is overcharged adiabatically at 1/3C to 1C. Thermal runaway temperature (TTR) remains near 113 °C and SOCTR decreases only slightly (1.674 to 1.652). DSC of overcharged cathodes shows no exothermic activity below 250 °C. Anode heat generation rises from 1592 J/g at SOC 1.00 to 2553 J/g at SOC 1.85, with lithium metal deposition beginning above SOC 1.25. Which statements are supported by these observations?
options: A. Cathode decomposition is the primary driver of heat generation before thermal runaway under these overcharge conditions.
B. Deposited lithium metal on the anode is more reactive than lithiated graphite at elevated temperature and accelerates battery temperature rise.
C. Charging rate between 1/3C and 1C is a dominant factor controlling the SOC at which thermal runaway occurs.
D. Anode-side reactions become increasingly significant as overcharge progresses beyond SOC 1.25.
E. The minor variation in SOCTR across charging rates implies that heat accumulation rate, not SOC, primarily governs thermal runaway onset under adiabatic conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cathodes show no exothermic behavior below 250 °C, well above the observed TTR < 150 °C; cathode contribution is explicitly ruled out.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Anode heat generation exceeds 1.5× initial value with lithium deposition; evidence directly states deposited lithium accelerates temperature rise.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SOCTR changes only from 1.674 to 1.652 across the tested rate range; evidence explicitly labels charging current influence as minor.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lithium deposition begins at SOC 1.25 and anode heat generation grows substantially thereafter, supporting increasing anode-side significance.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence notes TTR is stable and SOCTR varies little, but does not establish heat accumulation rate as the governing variable over SOC; this is an unsupported causal overclaim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing anode vs. cathode contributions to pre-TR heat generation and evaluating charging rate influence on SOCTR
analysis.primary_boundary: TTR < 150 °C; cathode exotherms only above 250 °C; charging rate range 1/3C–1C
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing cathode inactivity below 250 °C to all chemistries or temperatures
Extending minor SOCTR variation to claim rate is irrelevant at all C-rates

solver_view.prompt: A pouch lithium-ion battery is overcharged adiabatically. Two configurations are compared: Configuration A (no restraining plate, no pouch cuts) undergoes thermal runaway at TTR = 113.1 °C and SOCTR = 1.670. Configuration C (restraining plate plus pouch cuts) undergoes thermal runaway at TTR = 140.9 °C and SOCTR = 1.738. DSC of overcharged cathodes shows no exothermic activity below 250 °C. Lithium metal deposits on the anode above SOC 1.25, and anode heat generation rises to over 1.5× its initial value by SOC 1.85. Explain the dominant heat generation mechanism before TR in these overcharge tests and why cathode decomposition can be excluded as a significant contributor under these conditions.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Before thermal runaway, the dominant heat source is the anode, not the cathode. Overcharged cathodes show no measurable exothermic activity until approximately 250 °C, which is well above the observed TTR of less than 150 °C in all tests; cathode decomposition therefore cannot contribute meaningfully to heat generation before TR occurs. On the anode side, lithium metal begins depositing once SOC exceeds 1.25. Deposited lithium is more reactive than lithiated graphite at elevated temperatures, and anode heat generation increases to more than 1.5 times its initial value as SOC rises toward 1.85. This progressive increase in anode reactivity is the primary mechanism accelerating battery temperature rise during overcharge, ultimately driving the system to the TR threshold.
must_include: Cathode exotherms only above ~250 °C, above the observed TTR < 150 °C
Cathode decomposition excluded as significant pre-TR heat source
Lithium metal deposition on anode begins above SOC 1.25
Anode heat generation increases to more than 1.5× initial value
Deposited lithium more reactive than lithiated graphite at elevated temperature
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming cathode is inactive at all temperatures or in all chemistries
Stating anode reactions alone trigger TR without acknowledging the thermal threshold condition
Generalizing findings beyond the tested adiabatic overcharge conditions

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal reasoning linking SOC-dependent lithium deposition to anode heat generation increase, while excluding cathode contribution via temperature boundary argument
analysis.primary_boundary: TTR < 150 °C; cathode exotherm onset ~250 °C; lithium deposition threshold SOC 1.25; adiabatic overcharge only

solver_view.stem: A prismatic lithium-ion cell undergoes 0.5C overcharge under uniaxial constraint. The expansion force peaks at ~13,919 N before venting. At the moment the safety valve opens, strain hardening contributes +64.67 MPa, strain rate hardening contributes +22.15 MPa, and thermal softening reduces yield strength by 18.41 MPa. Which statements correctly characterize the mechanical state of the casing at safety valve activation?
options: A. The net yield strength change is positive, meaning the casing is mechanically stronger than its undeformed baseline at this moment.
B. Thermal softening is the dominant factor controlling yield strength at safety valve activation, exceeding combined strengthening by more than 20%.
C. Combined strengthening effects exceed thermal softening by more than 300%, indicating deformation-driven hardening dominates over temperature-driven weakening at this stage.
D. Battery surface temperature has already exceeded 200°C before the expansion force peak, driving the observed high expansion force.
E. The large expansion force before venting can occur while surface temperature remains below 50°C, indicating force is not a reliable proxy for surface thermal hazard at this stage.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Net change = +64.67 +22.15 -18.41 = +68.41 MPa > 0, so yield strength is above baseline.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal softening (18.41 MPa) is far smaller than combined strengthening (86.82 MPa); direction of dominance is reversed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states total strengthening is 371.59% greater than thermal softening at safety valve activation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states surface temperature remained below 50°C before the expansion force peak, contradicting >200°C claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Force peaked at ~13,919 N while surface temperature stayed below 50°C, decoupling force magnitude from surface thermal state.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanical state at safety valve activation: sign and dominance of yield strength contributors, and decoupling of expansion force from surface temperature.
analysis.primary_boundary: Conditions specific to safety valve activation moment during 0.5C overcharge; not generalizable to post-TR state.
disallowed_extrapolations: Post-TR softening dominance cannot be applied to pre-vent state.
High expansion force does not imply high surface temperature.

solver_view.stem: After thermal runaway in a 0.5C overcharge test, the casing yield strength reaches its minimum. At this point: strain hardening contributes +78.93 MPa, strain rate hardening contributes +25.34 MPa, and thermal softening reduces yield strength by 151.35 MPa. The cell is under uniaxial constraint. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. Post-TR thermal softening exceeds combined strengthening, reversing the mechanical dominance relationship observed at safety valve activation.
B. The weakening effect exceeds combined strengthening by approximately 45%, meaning the casing is at its structurally weakest relative state after TR.
C. Because strain hardening increased from 64.67 MPa to 78.93 MPa after TR, the casing is net stronger post-TR than at safety valve activation.
D. Under uniaxial constraint post-TR, maximum plastic strain concentrates at the top and bottom plate corners rather than the side walls.
E. The post-TR reversal of mechanical dominance implies that surface temperature also reverses its trend and drops below 50°C after TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Post-TR softening (151.35 MPa) exceeds combined strengthening (104.27 MPa), reversing the pre-vent dominance relationship.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states weakening exceeds combined strengthening by 45.15% at minimum yield strength after TR.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Net yield strength change post-TR = +78.93+25.34-151.35 = -47.08 MPa < 0; casing is net weaker despite higher strain hardening.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Under uniaxial constraint, side-wall suppression forces top/bottom plates to bulge, concentrating plastic strain at corner discontinuities.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence links post-TR mechanical reversal to a temperature drop; TR produces ~475°C, not sub-50°C conditions.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Post-TR mechanical dominance reversal and deformation localization under uniaxial constraint.
analysis.primary_boundary: Post-TR minimum yield strength state; uniaxial constraint geometry.
disallowed_extrapolations: Increased strain hardening magnitude does not imply net strengthening when softening is larger.
Mechanical reversal does not imply thermal reversal.

solver_view.stem: NCM811|Gr pouch cells are charged to 4.2 V using either a base electrolyte (BE) or a high-safety electrolyte (HSE). Thermal runaway is induced by external heating. The BE cell triggers thermal runaway at T2 = 223.6 °C with a peak temperature T3 = 715.4 °C. The HSE cell shows T2 = 248.0 °C and T3 = 537.7 °C. The incubation time extends from 8.03 × 10⁴ s (BE) to 9.25 × 10⁴ s (HSE). Which of the following conclusions are supported by these observations?
options: A. HSE raises the thermal runaway trigger temperature by approximately 24 °C relative to BE at 4.2 V.
B. HSE eliminates thermal runaway entirely in NCM811|Gr cells at 4.2 V.
C. HSE reduces the maximum temperature reached during thermal runaway by approximately 178 °C relative to BE at 4.2 V.
D. The extended incubation time with HSE indicates postponement of thermal decomposition onset.
E. HSE lowers T2 by suppressing cathode oxidation before the trigger point, as shown by the incubation data alone.
F. The reduction in T3 with HSE is consistent with reduced heat release during the runaway event.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T2 increases from 223.6 °C to 248.0 °C, a difference of 24.4 °C, directly supported by evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows TR still occurs with HSE at 4.2 V; only temperatures are shifted, not TR eliminated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T3 drops from 715.4 °C to 537.7 °C, a reduction of 177.7 °C, directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Longer incubation time is explicitly described as demonstrating postponement of thermal decomposition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Incubation time data alone cannot isolate cathode oxidation suppression; causal mechanism is overclaimed from timing data.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower T3 is consistent with reduced heat release; radical-quenching mechanism supports this inference.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing supported quantitative comparisons from overclaimed mechanistic or eliminative conclusions
analysis.primary_boundary: Observations are confined to 4.2 V, NCM811|Gr chemistry; HSE delays/reduces but does not eliminate TR
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing TR elimination to other voltages or chemistries
Attributing T2 shift solely to cathode effects from incubation data

solver_view.stem: NCM811|Gr pouch cells with base electrolyte (BE) show thermal runaway trigger temperature T2 = 223.6 °C at 4.2 V and T2 = 194.6 °C at 4.5 V (a drop of 29 °C). Cells with HSE show T2 = 248.0 °C at 4.2 V and T2 = 245.0 °C at 4.5 V. A ceramic-coated separator is present but thermal runaway still occurs. Which statements are consistent with these findings?
options: A. Increasing cut-off voltage from 4.2 V to 4.5 V significantly destabilizes BE-based cells, lowering T2 by ~29 °C.
B. HSE-based cells show comparable voltage sensitivity to BE-based cells when cut-off voltage increases from 4.2 V to 4.5 V.
C. The ceramic-coated separator prevents thermal runaway by blocking all chemical species transport between electrodes.
D. HSE confers near-voltage-insensitive T2 behavior between 4.2 V and 4.5 V, with T2 changing by only ~3 °C.
E. Reductive gas migration through separator pores can sustain thermal runaway even when direct electrode contact is blocked.
F. The 29 °C drop in T2 for BE cells at 4.5 V implies that BE cells are safer at higher voltages due to earlier venting.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T2 for BE drops from 223.6 °C to 194.6 °C across the voltage range, a 29 °C decrease, directly evidenced.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: BE drops 29 °C while HSE drops only 3 °C; their voltage sensitivities are markedly different, not comparable.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states TR still occurs because reactive species migrate through separator pores despite ceramic coating.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: HSE T2 changes only 3 °C (248.0 to 245.0 °C) across the voltage range, supporting near-voltage-insensitive behavior.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states reductive/oxide species migrate through separator holes, sustaining TR despite blocked direct contact.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Lower T2 means TR triggers at a lower temperature, indicating greater hazard, not improved safety; causal direction reversed.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Voltage sensitivity contrast between BE and HSE, and limitations of physical separator barriers
analysis.primary_boundary: Voltage range 4.2–4.5 V, NCM811|Gr chemistry; ceramic separator present but insufficient alone
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming ceramic separator eliminates TR
Equating lower T2 with improved safety

solver_view.stem: A 9-cell NCM811|Gr battery module simulation compares BE and HSE cells. BE cells show thermal runaway propagation at 29 s per cell with a module peak temperature of 730.6 °C. HSE cells self-quench after three cells trigger thermal runaway, with a module peak temperature of 202.7 °C. HSE has a self-extinguishing time (SET) of zero versus 29.3 s g⁻¹ for BE. PO• radicals from PFPN pyrolysis capture H• and OH• radicals. Which statements are supported?
options: A. In the HSE module, thermal runaway propagation arrests after three cells, preventing whole-module failure.
B. The non-flammable character of HSE, evidenced by SET = 0, contributes mechanistically to limiting module-level propagation.
C. PO• radical scavenging of H• and OH• impedes combustion chain reactions, consistent with reduced heat release at module scale.
D. HSE guarantees zero thermal runaway events at module level regardless of cell count or abuse severity.
E. The 29 s per cell propagation rate in BE modules implies that module failure is complete within approximately 4 minutes for a 9-cell pack.
F. Radical quenching by PO• is the sole mechanism responsible for the module-level temperature reduction observed with HSE.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states only three cells trigger TR and the module self-quenches, preventing full propagation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SET = 0 indicates non-flammable electrolyte; non-flammability mechanistically limits heat feeding propagation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: PO• captures H• and OH•, impeding chain reactions and reducing heat release, consistent with lower module peak temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Three cells still trigger TR in the HSE module; zero TR events is an unsupported universal claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 9 cells × 29 s per cell = 232 s ≈ 3.9 minutes; arithmetic is consistent with the stated propagation rate.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Non-flammability (SET = 0) and elevated T2 also contribute; attributing reduction solely to PO• scavenging is an unsupported single-cause claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Module-level propagation arrest mechanisms and admissibility of single-cause claims
analysis.primary_boundary: 9-cell simulation under defined abuse; HSE limits but does not eliminate TR at cell level
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming HSE prevents all TR events
Attributing module temperature reduction to a single mechanism

solver_view.stem: During thermal runaway of 16Ah NCM523 under external heating, simulation identifies sequential decomposition events. The SEI film begins decomposing at 94.1 °C, the positive active material reacts with electrolyte near 324 °C, and electrolyte decomposition occurs near 360 °C. The soft-package burst is experimentally observed at 96.4 °C. Which conclusions are consistent with both the simulation thresholds and experimental observations?
options: A The soft-package burst at 96.4 °C coincides with the onset of SEI film decomposition, suggesting SEI decomposition contributes to early mechanical failure.
B Positive active material reaction with electrolyte is the first exothermic event to occur after the soft-package burst under heating conditions.
C Electrolyte decomposition initiates before the positive active material reacts with electrolyte during heating TR.
D The maximum temperature of 420.2 °C exceeds the simulation thresholds for both positive-electrolyte reaction and electrolyte decomposition, meaning both reactions likely occurred during the event.
E SEI decomposition onset temperature alone is sufficient to predict the maximum temperature reached during heating TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI onset 94.1 °C and burst 96.4 °C are within ~2 °C; temporal and thermal proximity supports the association.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Burst at 96.4 °C precedes 324 °C threshold; positive-electrolyte reaction is the next simulation-identified exotherm post-burst.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Simulation places positive-electrolyte reaction at ~324 °C and electrolyte decomposition at ~360 °C; order is reversed in this option.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Maximum temperature 420.2 °C exceeds both 324 °C and 360 °C thresholds, consistent with both reactions having occurred.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEI onset temperature characterizes initiation only; maximum temperature depends on subsequent exotherms not captured by SEI threshold alone.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Sequential reaction thresholds vs. experimental temperature milestones in heating TR
analysis.primary_boundary: Simulation thresholds apply to heating TR only; causal sufficiency of individual thresholds is not supported
disallowed_extrapolations: Predicting maximum temperature from SEI onset alone
Transferring heating TR reaction sequence to other abuse modes

solver_view.stem: An NCM lithium-ion battery is aged by high-temperature cycling at 50°C. Post-aging characterization shows new XRD peaks in the cathode at 2θ = 38.88° and 44.8°, and EDS detects Mn on the anode surface. Simultaneously, SEI growth is confirmed on the anode. Which statements correctly describe the opposing thermal effects of these two aging mechanisms on battery safety during subsequent abuse?
options: A. SEI growth increases the onset temperature of thermal reactions, improving early-stage thermal stability.
B. Mn dissolution decreases the thermal runaway temperature, increasing hazard at the runaway stage.
C. SEI growth decreases onset temperature because the thicker SEI layer decomposes at lower temperatures.
D. Mn dissolution increases thermal runaway temperature by stabilizing the cathode crystal structure.
E. Both SEI growth and Mn dissolution independently increase thermal stability, producing a net safety improvement after high-temperature cycling.
F. The crystal structure change in the cathode caused by Mn dissolution is evidenced by the new XRD peaks and is mechanistically linked to the decrease in thermal runaway temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states SEI growth causes onset temperature to increase, improving resistance to early thermal reactions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states Mn dissolution causes thermal runaway temperature to decrease, increasing hazard.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: This reverses the reported trend; SEI growth increases, not decreases, onset temperature per evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Mn dissolution causes crystal structure change and decreases thermal runaway temperature; it does not stabilize the cathode.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Mn dissolution decreases thermal runaway temperature, so the two mechanisms have opposing not uniformly beneficial effects.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence links new XRD peaks to cathode crystal structure change caused by Mn dissolution, which in turn decreases thermal runaway temperature.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Opposing thermal effects of SEI growth vs. Mn dissolution on onset and runaway temperatures
analysis.primary_boundary: Applies to NCM cells after high-temperature cycling at 50°C; both mechanisms co-exist and have distinct temperature effects
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming net safety improvement from high-temperature cycling
Generalizing Mn dissolution effects to non-Mn-containing cathodes

solver_view.prompt: An NCM lithium-ion battery pack undergoes high-temperature cycling at 50°C. A safety engineer notes that capacity drops sharply near 90% SOH and that the 90% SOH condition is the most hazardous under external short circuit, despite 80% and 70% SOH batteries being safer. Explain the mechanistic reasons why 90% SOH represents a peak hazard point rather than a monotonically increasing or decreasing safety trend with degradation. Your answer should address at least two distinct heat generation mechanisms and how their relative contributions shift across SOH levels.

ground_truth.gold_answer: At 90% SOH, a combination of factors converges to create peak hazard. First, fast-discharging side reactions during ESC generate significant heat in aged batteries, and this effect is maximal near 90% SOH, decreasing as SOH falls further to 80% or 70%. Second, for 90% and 80% SOH batteries, heat from side reactions dominates total heat generation (rather than Joule heat), whereas at 70% SOH the heat profile reverts to resembling the fresh battery where Joule heat from polarization resistance dominates. Third, the sharp capacity degradation near 90% SOH reflects serious cathode and anode degradation, which amplifies side-reaction heat. At 70% SOH, the side-reaction heat contribution has diminished sufficiently that total heat no longer drives thermal runaway. Meanwhile, Joule heat is actually highest for the 100% SOH battery and does not drive the 90% SOH peak hazard. The result is a non-monotonic safety ranking: 70%>100%>80%>90% SOH.
must_include: fast-discharging side-reaction heat peaks at 90% SOH and decreases below 90% SOH
side reactions dominate heat generation at 90% and 80% SOH; Joule heat dominates at 100% and 70% SOH
non-monotonic safety ranking with 90% SOH as most hazardous
capacity drop near 90% SOH linked to serious cathode and anode degradation
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming resistance increase is the primary driver of hazard at 90% SOH
Asserting thermal runaway risk increases monotonically with degradation
Generalizing this non-monotonic pattern to other chemistries or aging modes without qualification

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Non-monotonic SOH safety ranking explained by shifting heat generation mechanism dominance across degradation levels
analysis.primary_boundary: NCM cells cycled at 50°C under hard ESC; findings specific to this chemistry and aging protocol

solver_view.prompt: A lithium-ion cell is overcharged until its SOC reaches 125%. An engineer notes that SEI crystal growth has physically damaged the separator. The engineer then compares this scenario to a mechanically damaged separator case and argues the two are equivalent hazards. Identify the key mechanistic difference that makes the overcharge scenario potentially more severe than simple mechanical separator damage, and explain the causal chain linking SOC level to separator failure in the overcharge case.

ground_truth.gold_answer: At SOC ≥ 120%, SEI crystals grow large enough to physically puncture the separator, which superficially resembles mechanical damage. However, the critical difference is that at SOC ≥ 100%, lithium-ion concentration near the electrode is already very high before separator failure occurs. This elevated concentration means that once the separator is breached, the electrochemical environment is far more reactive than in a purely mechanical damage scenario where SOC could be at any level. The causal chain is: overcharge raises SOC beyond 120% → SEI crystals grow to damaging size → separator is physically punctured → internal short occurs in a high-concentration, high-reactivity electrochemical environment, amplifying thermal runaway risk beyond what mechanical damage alone would produce.
must_include: SOC exceeds 120% as the threshold for SEI crystal growth sufficient to damage separator
High electrode concentration (SOC at or above 100%) as the distinguishing factor from mechanical damage
Physical separator damage by SEI crystals as the failure mechanism
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming overcharge always produces greater heat release than mechanical damage without referencing the concentration condition
Asserting SEI crystal damage occurs below 120% SOC
Generalizing the concentration effect to all overcharge scenarios regardless of SOC level

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanistic distinction between overcharge-induced separator failure and mechanical separator damage, grounded in SOC-dependent electrode concentration
analysis.primary_boundary: SOC > 120% threshold for SEI crystal growth; SOC ≥ 100% as the concentration condition that differentiates overcharge from mechanical damage
intended_error_modes: regime transfer error—treating overcharge and mechanical damage as equivalent
causal overclaim—ignoring the concentration precondition when assessing severity

solver_view.stem: A pristine NMC cell and a pristine LFP cell are subjected to identical external heating tests. The NMC cell reaches a maximum average temperature of ~753°C with a temperature rise rate of ~3100°C/min and accumulated heat release of ~28 kJ. The LFP cell's maximum temperature stays below 315°C and does not sustain self-sustaining exothermic reactions. Which of the following statements correctly explain the observed difference in thermal behavior?
options: A The LFP cathode decomposition reaction is endothermic, requiring energy input rather than releasing it, which limits heat accumulation.
B LFP releases approximately half the oxygen during cathode decomposition compared to NMC, reducing the heat released when oxygen reacts with electrolyte.
C The LFP separator melts at a higher temperature than the NMC separator, preventing internal short circuit.
D NMC cathode decomposition releases more oxygen, which reacts exothermically with electrolyte, amplifying heat generation.
E LFP cells cannot reach SEI decomposition onset (~100°C) under external heating, so no exothermic reactions occur at all.
F The higher thermal stability of LFP is solely due to its lower nominal voltage, which reduces Joule heating during short circuit.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states LFP cathode decomposition is endothermic, directly limiting heat release during thermal stress.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states LFP releases about half the oxygen of layered metal oxide cathodes like NMC, reducing electrolyte reaction heat.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Separator melt temperature is a chemistry-independent material property (PP/PE); no evidence links it to NMC vs LFP difference.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Greater oxygen release from NMC cathode reacts with electrolyte exothermically, consistent with evidence explaining NMC's higher heat release.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not claim LFP cells bypass SEI decomposition; it states LFP lacks sufficient self-sustaining reactions, not that early exothermic steps are absent.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Voltage-driven Joule heating is not cited as the mechanism; thermal safety is attributed to cathode endothermic decomposition and lower oxygen release.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Causal attribution of LFP vs NMC thermal runaway severity difference
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanism limited to cathode oxygen release and endothermic decomposition; not separator material or voltage
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming LFP never reaches SEI decomposition temperatures
Attributing difference to separator melting point differences

solver_view.stem: NMC cells are cycled at 50°C until significant capacity fading occurs, then subjected to thermal runaway testing. Compared to pristine NMC cells, the aged cells show lower maximum temperature and lower temperature rise rate, but thermal runaway is triggered at lower onset and crucial temperatures. An engineer proposes that aging always makes batteries safer because heat release decreases. Which options correctly identify flaws or limits in this reasoning?
options: A Aging at 50°C reduces heat release during thermal runaway due to SEI growth consuming lithium and electrolyte, so total energy release is lower — this part of the engineer's claim is supported.
B Lower onset temperature in aged cells means thermal runaway is triggered more easily, contradicting the claim that aging uniformly improves safety.
C Aging at 20°C produces similar capacity fading and heat release reduction as aging at 50°C, so temperature of aging is irrelevant to safety outcomes.
D The aging model only accounts for SEI growth; lithium plating and material cracking are excluded, limiting applicability to low-temperature or high-current aging conditions.
E Because LFP aged cells show 68.9% heat release reduction versus 12.3% for NMC, the safety improvement from aging is chemistry-independent.
F Reduced heat release in aged NMC does not eliminate thermal runaway; the process is still triggered, just at lower onset temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence confirms SEI growth consumes lithium and electrolyte, reducing heat released in anode-electrolyte reaction for aged cells.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states aged NMC at 50°C shows lower onset and crucial temperatures, meaning runaway triggers more easily despite lower peak heat.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states 20°C aging yields similar heat release to pristine batteries, while 50°C aging yields greater reduction — temperature of aging is highly relevant.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states the model excludes lithium plating and cracking, restricting validity to high-temperature aging scenarios only.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Different percentage reductions (12.3% NMC vs 68.9% LFP) demonstrate chemistry-dependent outcomes, directly contradicting chemistry-independence claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence confirms aged NMC still undergoes thermal runaway; lower heat release does not prevent triggering, only reduces peak severity metrics.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-dimensional safety trade-off in aged NMC cells: reduced heat release vs. lower onset temperature
analysis.primary_boundary: Aging model scope limited to SEI growth under high-temperature conditions; 20°C vs 50°C aging produce distinct outcomes
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming aging universally improves safety
Treating chemistry-specific aging outcomes as universal

solver_view.stem: During external heating of a lithium-ion cell, which sequence of thermal events is consistent with the described mechanisms? Assume a polypropylene/polyethylene separator and that temperature rises continuously from ambient. Identify which statements about the ordered sequence are INCORRECT.
options: A SEI decomposition begins near 100°C, releasing heat before separator failure occurs.
B Separator melting at 120–130°C precedes SEI decomposition onset, initiating the first exothermic event.
C After separator melting, direct anode-cathode contact creates a short circuit, generating additional Joule heating.
D SEI decomposition at ~100°C immediately causes cathode oxygen release, which is the dominant first-stage heat source.
E The short circuit caused by separator melt produces heat via Joule's law, which can further elevate internal temperature.
F Separator integrity is maintained until well above 200°C in all lithium-ion chemistries regardless of separator material.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence places SEI decomposition at ~100°C and separator melt at 120–130°C, confirming SEI events precede separator failure.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states SEI decomposition (~100°C) precedes separator melt (120–130°C); this option reverses the correct order.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states separator melt removes anode-cathode protection, causing short circuit consistent with this description.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes first heat release to SEI decomposition, not cathode oxygen release; cathode decomposition is a later, distinct stage.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states short circuit produces more heat according to Joule's law, consistent with this option.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence specifies PP/PE separators melt at 120–130°C; claiming integrity holds well above 200°C universally contradicts stated thresholds.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Correct ordering of thermal runaway event sequence and first-stage heat source identification
analysis.primary_boundary: SEI decomposition at ~100°C precedes separator melt at 120–130°C; first heat attributed to SEI not cathode
disallowed_extrapolations: Treating cathode oxygen release as the first thermal event
Claiming separator integrity extends universally beyond stated melt range

solver_view.prompt: An engineer tests two NMC battery populations: one pristine and one aged at 50°C until measurable capacity fading. During thermal runaway testing, the aged population releases less accumulated heat and reaches a lower maximum temperature than the pristine population, but thermal runaway onset occurs at a lower temperature in the aged cells. The engineer concludes that aging at 50°C unambiguously improves NMC battery safety. Identify the mechanistic basis for the reduced heat release, explain why the engineer's safety conclusion is incomplete, and state one scope limitation of the aging analysis that prevents generalizing these results to all use conditions.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Reduced heat release in aged NMC cells results from SEI growth during cycling at 50°C, which consumes lithium and electrolyte in the anode, leaving fewer reactants available for the anode-electrolyte exothermic reaction during thermal runaway. The safety conclusion is incomplete because, despite lower peak heat release, aged cells are triggered into thermal runaway at lower onset and crucial temperatures, meaning the hazard threshold is reached more easily — a safety-relevant trade-off the engineer ignores. The scope limitation is that the aging model considers only SEI growth as the capacity-fading mechanism; lithium plating and material cracking are excluded because they dominate under low-temperature operation or very high current demands, so results cannot be generalized to those conditions.
must_include: SEI growth consumes lithium and electrolyte reducing anode-electrolyte reaction heat
lower onset temperature means thermal runaway triggered more easily in aged cells
aging model excludes lithium plating and cracking limiting applicability to low-temperature or high-current conditions
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming aging always improves safety across all chemistries or aging temperatures
Stating LFP and NMC show equivalent aging-induced heat release reductions
Generalizing 50°C aging results to 20°C aging conditions without noting the evidence shows 20°C aging yields heat release similar to pristine

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanistic cause of heat release reduction in aged NMC and multi-dimensional safety trade-off including onset temperature shift and model scope
analysis.primary_boundary: Aging model valid only for high-temperature SEI-growth-dominated aging; onset temperature reduction contradicts simple safety improvement narrative
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim on universal safety improvement
scope transfer error to low-temperature or high-current aging

solver_view.stem: A cell charged at -5°C almost immediately enters constant-voltage (CV) mode. Separately, metallic lithium dendrite growth has been identified as a pathway to thermal runaway. A cell cycled at 25°C shows capacity increase up to ~41 cycles before declining. Which of the following statements are NOT supported by the evidence?
options: A. Early CV entry at -5°C is caused solely by slow charge transfer kinetics.
B. Dendrite-induced separator puncture is a plausible thermal runaway initiation pathway in low-temperature cycled cells.
C. The capacity rise at 25°C before cycle 41 is attributable to improved electrolyte access following crystallite cracking.
D. Slow solid-state lithium deposition contributes to early CV mode entry at -5°C.
E. Low-temperature cycling poses no additional thermal runaway risk compared to ambient cycling because SEI resistance does not change significantly.
F. Early CV mode entry at -5°C guarantees thermal runaway within 41 cycles.

option_judgments.label: Correct (NOT supported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence cites three concurrent causes: slow solid-state deposition, slow bulk electrolyte transport, and slow charge transfer; sole attribution is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (IS supported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states dendrite growth can puncture separator and is responsible for thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (IS supported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly attributes early capacity rise at 25°C to crystallite cracking improving electrolyte access to active material.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (IS supported)
option_judgments.justification: Evidence lists slow solid-state lithium deposition as one explicit contributor to high resistance and early CV entry at -5°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct (NOT supported)
option_judgments.justification: Unchanged SEI resistance does not negate dendrite-related thermal runaway risk; Rs growth and lithium deposition still elevate risk.

option_judgments.label: Correct (NOT supported)
option_judgments.justification: No evidence links CV mode entry timing to a guaranteed thermal runaway cycle count; this is an unsupported causal overclaim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying unsupported causal overclaims and boundary overreaches in multi-mechanism degradation scenarios
analysis.primary_boundary: Negation-framed stem requiring polarity discipline; SEI stability does not imply overall safety equivalence
disallowed_extrapolations: Single-cause attribution for multi-factor CV entry
SEI stability as proxy for total thermal runaway risk

solver_view.prompt: A lithium-ion cell cycled at -5°C shows 73% series resistance growth, 16% charge transfer resistance growth, no significant SEI resistance change, and an anode lithium deposit area fraction of 8.6% (vs 4.5% at 25°C). Explain the mechanistic chain by which low-temperature cycling produces these impedance signatures, and identify which resistance component is most diagnostic of the dominant degradation mechanism. Also state one thermal runaway implication supported by these findings.

ground_truth.gold_answer: At -5°C, reduced electrolyte conductivity and slowed intercalation rate promote lithium metal deposition on the anode surface instead of intercalation. Dead lithium can detach from the anode into the electrolyte, physically restricting ion flow and driving the large Rs growth (73%). Because dead lithium affects bulk electrolyte transport rather than the electrode-electrolyte interface, SEI resistance remains unchanged and Rct growth is modest (16%). Rs is therefore the most diagnostic component, reflecting electrolyte-phase ion-flow restriction. The nearly doubled anode deposit fraction at -5°C also increases the risk of metallic lithium dendrite growth, which can puncture the separator and initiate thermal runaway.
must_include: reduced electrolyte conductivity and/or slowed intercalation rate at low temperature
dead lithium detachment into electrolyte restricts ion flow, explaining Rs dominance
SEI resistance unchanged because dead lithium mechanism does not thicken SEI
Rs identified as most diagnostic resistance component
dendrite growth as thermal runaway pathway via separator puncture
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming SEI resistance grows at low temperature
Attributing Rs growth primarily to SEI or charge transfer mechanisms
Asserting thermal runaway is inevitable or occurs within a specific cycle count
Generalizing TM dissolution as a low-temperature risk

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-step causal chain from low-temperature kinetics to impedance signatures to thermal runaway implication
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanism specificity: Rs reflects electrolyte-phase restriction by dead lithium, not SEI growth; safety implication limited to dendrite-separator pathway
intended_error_modes: regime transfer error (applying TM dissolution to low-temperature risk)

solver_view.stem: During thermal runaway progression in a lithium-ion pouch cell, an engineer claims: 'SEI decomposition is the first side reaction to occur as cell temperature rises, with a possible onset near 57°C, and the metastable SEI components transform to stable components between 90–120°C while releasing heat.' A colleague argues this sequence is INCORRECT in at least one respect. Which options identify statements that are NOT supported or are contradicted by the evidence?
options: A SEI decomposition onset near 57°C is a well-established universal threshold applicable to all lithium-ion cell chemistries.
B Metastable SEI components (ROCO2Li, ROLi) transform to stable components (LiF, Li2CO3) in the 90–120°C range while releasing heat.
C The first side reaction during thermal runaway is SEI decomposition, occurring at 80–120°C.
D SEI decomposition beginning below 60°C means it cannot also be described as occurring at 80–120°C; the two temperature ranges are mutually exclusive descriptions of different phenomena.
E Slow SEI decomposition may begin near 57°C, but the formal characterization of metastable SEI decomposition as the first side reaction in thermal runaway is anchored to the 80–120°C range.

option_judgments.label: Correct (NOT supported)
option_judgments.justification: C004 cites 57°C as a literature-derived possible onset; C011 limits conclusions to the specific cells tested, not all chemistries.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (IS supported)
option_judgments.justification: C005 explicitly states metastable-to-stable SEI transformation occurs at 90–120°C with heat release.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (IS supported)
option_judgments.justification: C003 states SEI decomposition at 80–120°C is the first side reaction during thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct (NOT supported)
option_judgments.justification: C003 and C004 together describe slow onset below 60°C and formal TR reaction range of 80–120°C as complementary, not mutually exclusive.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (IS supported)
option_judgments.justification: C003 and C004 together support this reconciliation: slow decomposition below 60°C and formal TR first-reaction range at 80–120°C coexist.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Correct interpretation of SEI decomposition temperature ranges and universality limits
analysis.primary_boundary: Cell-type specificity of 57°C onset; compatibility of sub-60°C slow onset with 80–120°C formal TR classification
disallowed_extrapolations: Treating 57°C onset as universal
Treating two temperature descriptions as contradictory rather than complementary

solver_view.prompt: Pouch cells aged at 60°C and 80°C for 100 cycles show very different thermal runaway characteristics despite both experiencing significant capacity loss (SOH 33% and 8% respectively). Using the evidence about SEI evolution, gas generation, aluminium-plastic film integrity, and heat release, explain why the 80°C-aged cell exhibits a longer thermal runaway duration but lower maximum temperature compared to the 60°C-aged cell, and identify the primary factor governing the large difference in heat release between these two aging conditions.

ground_truth.gold_answer: At 60°C aging, SEI decomposition products accumulate as a thicker SEI layer, preserving cell integrity; thermal runaway proceeds with shorter duration and higher maximum temperature. At 80°C, SEI decomposition products transform into loose-structured particles, generating large amounts of gas that rupture the aluminium-plastic film and cause electrolyte evaporation. Electrolyte loss extends thermal runaway duration by reducing peak reaction rate while capping maximum temperature. The ~40% reduction in heat release for 80°C-aged cells versus 25°C-aged cells is primarily attributed not to aging temperature itself but to whether the aluminium-plastic film ruptured during aging, since film rupture causes electrolyte loss before the thermal runaway test, directly reducing available reactive material.
must_include: loose-structured SEI particles at 80°C generate gas causing film rupture
electrolyte evaporation after film rupture lowers maximum temperature and extends duration
heat release difference is governed by film rupture during aging, not aging temperature per se
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming SOH alone determines heat release magnitude
Generalizing these film-rupture effects to non-pouch or other cell chemistries
Stating 80°C aging universally applies to all lithium-ion cell types

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Causal chain linking SEI morphology change at 80°C to gas generation, film rupture, electrolyte loss, and resulting TR characteristics
analysis.primary_boundary: Heat release governed by film rupture, not aging temperature directly; findings limited to tested cell type
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim attributing heat release to SOH
scale transfer error generalizing to all pouch cells
mechanism substitution replacing film rupture with SEI thickness as primary factor

solver_view.stem: A module CFD simulation omits vent-gas combustion. During propagation, average internal air temperature rises from 25°C to ~500°C at initial thermal runaway onset and exceeds 800°C after all cells react. Insulation pads between select cell pairs extend inter-cell runaway intervals to 72–75 s versus 8–32 s for non-padded pairs. Which conclusions are NOT supported by this evidence?
options: A. The simulation likely underestimates peak module temperatures because combustion heat is excluded.
B. Insulation pads eliminate thermal runaway propagation between padded cell pairs.
C. Vent gas convection is a significant driver of internal module temperature rise.
D. The simulation results can be directly applied to predict absolute peak temperatures in real modules with combustion.
E. Insulation pads delay but do not prevent thermal runaway propagation based on the reported time intervals.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Excluding combustion is an identified limitation that may underestimate heat, supporting this conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (unsupported)
option_judgments.justification: Padded pairs still experience propagation; intervals are longer but propagation occurs, so elimination is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Temperature rise from 25°C to 500°C driven by vent gas convection is directly reported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect (unsupported)
option_judgments.justification: Combustion omission means simulation underestimates real heat; direct application to real modules is an unsupported overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Propagation still occurs across padded pairs (intervals reported), confirming delay not prevention.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of conclusions from a combustion-omitting module simulation with insulation pads
analysis.primary_boundary: Simulation excludes combustion; insulation pads delay not prevent propagation
intended_error_modes: boundary overreach claiming elimination of propagation
scale transfer overclaim applying simulation temperatures to real combustion-inclusive modules
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming simulation results bound real peak temperatures from above
Inferring insulation pads provide complete thermal isolation

solver_view.stem: A fully charged NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC pouch cell undergoes ARC testing. The aluminum plastic film ruptures at 129.6°C, and thermal runaway is ultimately triggered at 249°C, reaching a peak of 490.9°C. A fully charged AC//HC Li-HSC tested under identical ARC conditions shows a maximum temperature rise rate of 5.5°C min⁻¹. Which of the following statements are supported by these observations?
options: A The NCM component introduction is the root cause of thermal runaway in the hybrid supercapacitor, not the carbon-based electrodes alone.
B The AC//HC Li-HSC undergoes thermal runaway at a lower threshold than the NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC due to absence of O2-releasing cathode.
C The film rupture at 129.6°C causes a temporary reduction in temperature rise rate by releasing gases including vaporized electrolyte solvent.
D The NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC catches fire during thermal runaway because peak temperature exceeds 490°C.
E The 5.5°C min⁻¹ maximum rate in AC//HC Li-HSC is insufficient to meet the 1°C s⁻¹ thermal runaway threshold, confirming no thermal runaway occurred.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states NCM introduction is the rooted thermal runaway trigger; AC//HC alone does not exhibit thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: AC//HC Li-HSC does NOT undergo thermal runaway at all; claiming a lower threshold inverts the finding entirely.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states gas release including vaporized electrolyte after film rupture at 129.6°C reduces reaction and temperature rise rate.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC does not catch fire or explode during thermal runaway despite high peak temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 5.5°C min⁻¹ equals ~0.092°C s⁻¹, well below the 1°C s⁻¹ standard; evidence confirms thermal runaway did not occur in AC//HC Li-HSC.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing thermal runaway causation by NCM component versus carbon-only configuration, and applying quantitative threshold judgment
analysis.primary_boundary: Thermal runaway threshold of 1°C s⁻¹ and the role of NCM as trigger; AC//HC alone never reaches thermal runaway
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing AC//HC thermal runaway to other chemistries
Assuming high peak temperature alone implies fire or explosion

solver_view.stem: During ARC-induced thermal runaway of a fully charged NCM/AC//HC Li-HSC, gas analysis shows the O2/N2 ratio exceeds that of air. At peak temperature (Tpeak ≈ 490.9°C), the fraction of hydrocarbon gases and O2 decreases while CO and CO2 fractions increase. The PET/ceramic separator withstands thermolysis to 350°C, whereas separator collapse is observed at approximately 190°C triggering internal short circuit. Which statements correctly describe the gas evolution and separator behavior?
options: A The elevated O2/N2 ratio above air levels originates from decomposition of the NCM cathode and SEI film.
B Hydrocarbon gas fraction increases at Tpeak because NCM decomposition accelerates organic gas release at high temperature.
C CO and CO2 increase at Tpeak primarily due to oxidation reactions between released O2 and carbonate solvent.
D The PET/ceramic separator collapse at 190°C is consistent with its thermolysis limit of 350°C because mechanical collapse and thermolysis are distinct failure modes.
E The internal short circuit at separator collapse instantaneously releases stored electrical energy, generating rapid heat and temperature rise.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes the high O2/N2 ratio to decomposition of NCM cathode and SEI film during thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states hydrocarbon fraction decreases at Tpeak; attributing an increase to NCM decomposition reverses the reported trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly identifies oxidation reactions with carbonate solvent as the main reason for CO and CO2 increase at Tmax.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Thermolysis (chemical decomposition) and mechanical collapse are distinct; 190°C collapse does not contradict 350°C thermolysis resistance.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states internal short circuit instantaneously releases electrical energy generating great heat and rapid temperature increase.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Gas composition evolution mechanism at Tpeak and separator dual failure mode distinction
analysis.primary_boundary: Hydrocarbon fraction decreases (not increases) at Tpeak; thermolysis limit ≠ mechanical collapse temperature
intended_error_modes: mechanism conflation of thermolysis with mechanical collapse
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing separator behavior to PE/PP separators without evidence
Claiming O2 source is solely SEI without NCM contribution

solver_view.prompt: A researcher proposes that replacing the NCM/AC composite cathode with a pure AC cathode in a Li hybrid supercapacitor will eliminate thermal runaway risk entirely, because the carbon-based electrodes do not release O2. Using the ARC test evidence for both NCM/AC//HC and AC//HC Li-HSC configurations, evaluate this proposal. Address: (1) what evidence supports the reduced hazard claim, (2) what mechanistic role O2 plays, and (3) whether 'elimination of thermal runaway risk' is a fully supported conclusion or an overclaim.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Evidence supports reduced hazard: the AC//HC Li-HSC shows a maximum temperature rise rate of only 5.5°C min⁻¹ (≈0.092°C s⁻¹), below the 1°C s⁻¹ thermal runaway threshold, and thermal runaway was not observed under identical ARC conditions. Mechanistically, O2 released by NCM cathode decomposition accelerates self-heating reactions and is identified as the root trigger of thermal runaway in the NCM/AC//HC configuration; without NCM, this O2 source is absent. However, 'complete elimination of risk' is an overclaim: the evidence only shows thermal runaway did not occur under the specific ARC test conditions tested. Other abuse conditions, different SOC levels, or different cell formats are outside the tested scope and cannot be ruled out from this evidence alone.
must_include: 5.5°C min⁻¹ does not meet 1°C s⁻¹ threshold
O2 from NCM decomposition accelerates self-heating and is the root trigger
thermal runaway elimination claim is an overclaim beyond tested conditions
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming AC//HC is safe under all abuse conditions
Asserting O2 is the only possible thermal runaway trigger in any Li-HSC
Extending ARC results to nail, crush, or overcharge abuse modes

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Boundary-aware admissibility judgment on thermal runaway elimination claim combining quantitative threshold and mechanistic O2 role
analysis.primary_boundary: ARC test scope only; 1°C s⁻¹ threshold; NCM as specific O2 source and root trigger
intended_error_modes: scope transfer overclaim (safe under all conditions)
causal universalization (O2 is always the only trigger)

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell with a spinel manganese cathode and a graphite anode is cycled at elevated temperature in a fully charged state. Which of the following consequences are directly supported by the described conditions? Select all that apply.
options: A Manganese dissolution from the cathode is maximized under these conditions, as elevated temperature and charged state are identified as the most significant drivers.
B Dissolved manganese ions migrate to the anode, deposit on the electrode surface or within the SEI layer, and intensify electrolyte decomposition causing charge loss.
C SEI layer growth on the graphite anode is accelerated because elevated temperature expedites SEI formation regardless of anode potential.
D Lithium plating initiates automatically at elevated temperature because high temperature alone drives anode potential below 0 V vs. Li/Li+.
E The Arrhenius relationship implies that a 10°C temperature rise approximately doubles the degradation rate, compounding the manganese-dissolution-driven capacity loss.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states manganese dissolution is most significant for spinel cathodes at elevated temperatures and charged states.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states dissolved Mn migrates to anode, deposits in SEI, intensifies electrolyte decomposition, and causes charge loss in lithiated carbon anodes.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states SEI growth is expedited at lower anode potentials AND elevated temperatures; temperature alone is insufficient per the stated condition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Lithium plating requires anode potential to drop below 0 V vs. Li/Li+; elevated temperature alone does not guarantee this threshold is crossed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states a 10°C rise doubles degradation rate via Arrhenius, which compounds any concurrent degradation mechanism including Mn dissolution.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Condition-specific degradation mechanism chaining: Mn dissolution conditions + SEI acceleration conditions + Arrhenius scaling
analysis.primary_boundary: Elevated temperature and charged state are necessary conditions for peak Mn dissolution; SEI acceleration requires both low anode potential and elevated temperature
disallowed_extrapolations: Temperature alone drives lithium plating
SEI growth depends only on temperature

solver_view.stem: A trilayer PP/PE/PP separator is used in a cell that experiences a slow internal temperature rise. An engineer claims the separator provides a two-stage impedance response. A second engineer claims separator pore clogging from electrolyte decomposition products contributes roughly 10% of total cell impedance rise under normal aging. Which statements are consistent with the evidence?
options: A The trilayer separator impedance begins rising near 130°C due to PE layer melting and continues until approximately 165°C where PP melts, supporting a two-stage response.
B Separator pore clogging from electrolyte decomposition products accounts for approximately 10% of total cell impedance rise, consistent with the second engineer's claim.
C Separator pore closure at 130–150°C permanently prevents thermal runaway by stopping all electrochemical reactions irreversibly.
D The separator contributes approximately 15% of overall cell impedance increase during aging at elevated temperatures, a distinct figure from the 10% pore-clogging contribution.
E Because PE melts first, the separator loses all mechanical integrity at 130°C, making the PP layers irrelevant to subsequent impedance behavior.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states impedance increase begins at PE melting point (~130°C) and proceeds until PP melting point (~165°C) for a PP/PE/PP trilayer.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states electrolyte decomposition product pore clogging accounts for almost 10% of total cell impedance rise.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states pore closure stops charge/discharge by hindering ion transport but does not claim this irreversibly prevents thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence separately states separator accounts for ~15% of overall impedance increase during aging, distinct from the 10% pore-clogging figure.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence describes a two-stage response up to PP melting at 165°C, implying PP layers remain mechanically relevant after PE melting at 130°C.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing two numerically similar but mechanistically distinct impedance contributions from the separator; two-stage thermal response reasoning
analysis.primary_boundary: 10% figure applies to pore clogging under aging; 15% applies to overall separator impedance contribution; 130–165°C range applies only to trilayer PP/PE/PP
disallowed_extrapolations: Separator pore closure universally prevents thermal runaway
PE melting eliminates PP layer function

solver_view.stem: During deep discharge, a lithium-ion cell voltage drops to approximately 1 V. An engineer evaluates failure risks to the copper current collector and anode. Which of the following statements about the resulting degradation mechanisms are supported? Identify all correct options.
options: A At ~1 V cell voltage, copper current collector oxidizes and dissolves into the electrolyte as copper ions.
B During subsequent recharging after deep discharge, dissolved copper ions plate as copper metal onto the anode surface.
C Copper susceptibility to environmentally assisted cracking can be eliminated by controlling metallurgical conditions, specifically avoiding large grain size combined with work-hardening.
D Copper dissolution at ~1 V is driven by the same mechanism as lithium plating, since both require anode potential to drop below 0 V vs. Li/Li+.
E The volume change in the graphite anode from lithium insertion/extraction exceeds 10% under deep discharge conditions, accelerating copper collector delamination.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states copper collector oxidizes and dissolves at cell voltages around 1 V.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states dissolved copper ions plate as copper metal onto the anode during subsequent recharging.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states copper EAC susceptibility can be eliminated by excluding large grain size and work-hardening through metallurgical control.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Copper dissolution is triggered by cell voltage (~1 V), not by anode potential crossing 0 V vs. Li/Li+; these are distinct threshold mechanisms.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states graphite volume change is less than 10%; claiming it exceeds 10% under deep discharge contradicts the stated parameter boundary.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Deep-discharge copper dissolution mechanism and its recharging consequence; distinguishing copper EAC metallurgical control from electrochemical thresholds
analysis.primary_boundary: Copper dissolution threshold is ~1 V cell voltage, not 0 V vs. Li/Li+ anode potential; graphite volume change <10%
disallowed_extrapolations: Copper dissolution and lithium plating share the same threshold
Graphite volume change exceeds 10% under any condition

solver_view.stem: LiFePO4/graphite cells aged to different SOH levels (100%, 75%, 55%) are overcharged at 1C and subjected to post-ISC thermal analysis. SEI decomposition heat (QSEI) increases from 11.5 J·g⁻¹ at 100% SOH to 97.6 J·g⁻¹ at 55% SOH. Heat from lithiated graphite–binder reaction (QLi-bin) is 259.0 J·g⁻¹ at 75% SOH and drops to 51.2 J·g⁻¹ at 55% SOH. TR onset temperature is lowest at 70% SOH. Which conclusions are supported?
options: A. The increase in QSEI with aging is caused by SEI film thickening due to repeated rupture and repair cycles.
B. QLi-bin decreases from 75% to 55% SOH because lithium content in graphite decreases with aging.
C. Lower TR onset temperature at 70% SOH means 70% SOH batteries are definitively safer than 100% SOH batteries under all abuse conditions.
D. Post-ISC TR behavior differences across SOH levels are jointly governed by QSEI + QLi-ele and QLi-bin.
E. QSEI monotonically increases across all SOH levels from 100% to 55%, so the 55% SOH cell always has the highest TR severity.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes QSEI increase to SEI film thickening from continuous rupture and repair during aging.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states QLi-bin decrease from 75% to 55% SOH is due to decreased lithium content in graphite.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows lowest TR onset at 70% SOH indicating higher TR proneness, not greater safety; universal safety claim is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states QSEI + QLi-ele and QLi-bin together determine post-ISC TR behavior differences across SOH levels.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows QSEI increases with aging but QLi-bin decreases; TR severity depends on combined heat terms, not QSEI alone.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Competing aging effects on post-ISC heat release: QSEI increases while QLi-bin decreases, requiring joint evaluation for TR severity.
analysis.primary_boundary: SOH range 55%–100%, 1C overcharge, LiFePO4/graphite chemistry only.
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim (C: lowest onset temperature misread as safer)
unsupported monotonicity (E: single heat term used to infer overall TR severity)
disallowed_extrapolations: Extending SOH-dependent trends to other cell chemistries
Inferring absolute TR severity from QSEI alone without QLi-bin and QLi-ele

solver_view.stem: In a 100% SOH LiFePO4/graphite cell overcharged at 1C, heat from the lithiated graphite–binder reaction (QLi-bin) is 102.6 J·g⁻¹ at 100% SOC, rises to 228.5 J·g⁻¹ at 120% SOC, then falls to 182.0 J·g⁻¹ at 130% SOC. An ISC occurs at ~130% SOC. Which of the following correctly explain the non-monotonic QLi-bin trend?
options: A. QLi-bin rises from 100% to 120% SOC because additional lithium intercalates into graphite during overcharge, increasing reactant availability.
B. QLi-bin drops from 120% to 130% SOC because ISC at ~130% SOC consumes lithium from graphite, reducing reactant for the binder reaction.
C. QLi-bin drops from 120% to 130% SOC because separator pore closure at 130% SOC blocks lithium transport to the anode.
D. The QLi-bin peak at 120% SOC coincides with maximum lithium loading in graphite before ISC-induced lithium loss.
E. QLi-bin would continue rising beyond 130% SOC if ISC did not occur, based on the 100%–120% SOC trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Overcharge drives additional Li into graphite; higher lithium content increases QLi-bin, consistent with the 100%→120% SOC rise.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states QLi-bin decrease at 130% SOC is caused by ISC-induced reduction of lithium content in graphite.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Separator pore reduction occurs at 120% SOC, not 130%; evidence attributes the 130% SOC QLi-bin drop to ISC-driven lithium loss, not transport blockage.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The 120% SOC peak represents maximum graphite lithiation before ISC depletes lithium, making it the reactant-maximum point.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Extrapolating the rising trend beyond 130% SOC ignores ISC as the mechanism causing the reversal; this is an unsupported monotonicity claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Non-monotonic QLi-bin trend: rising phase driven by lithium loading, falling phase driven by ISC-induced lithium depletion.
analysis.primary_boundary: 100% SOH cell, 1C overcharge; ISC event at ~130% SOC is the mechanistic pivot.
intended_error_modes: mechanism substitution (C: separator pore closure misattributed as cause of QLi-bin drop)
unsupported monotonicity (E: extrapolating rising trend past ISC event)
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming QLi-bin trend generalizes to aged cells without accounting for reduced lithium content at lower SOH

solver_view.prompt: A battery engineer compares two LiFePO4/graphite cells overcharged at 1C: one at 100% SOH and one at 70% SOH. The 70% SOH cell shows the lowest thermal runaway onset temperature among cells tested from 100% to 60% SOH. The engineer proposes that because aged cells have higher QSEI (SEI decomposition heat) but lower QLi-bin (lithiated graphite–binder reaction heat) than fresh cells, the net thermal hazard of aged cells is always lower than fresh cells. Identify the flaw in this reasoning and explain what evidence-supported factors must be considered to properly assess TR hazard across SOH levels.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The flaw is treating QSEI and QLi-bin as the only two competing terms and assuming their net effect monotonically reduces hazard with aging. First, the engineer ignores QLi-ele (lithium–electrolyte reaction heat), which evidence identifies as jointly governing post-ISC TR behavior alongside QSEI and QLi-bin. Second, the lowest TR onset temperature at 70% SOH indicates that 70% SOH cells enter thermal runaway more readily, not that they are safer. TR hazard depends on both onset temperature and total heat release. Third, QLi-bin does decrease from 75% to 55% SOH, but QSEI increases substantially (11.5 to 97.6 J·g⁻¹), and the combined effect of all three heat terms determines TR severity. A proper assessment requires evaluating QSEI + QLi-ele + QLi-bin together across SOH levels, not comparing single terms in isolation.
must_include: QLi-ele is a required third heat term alongside QSEI and QLi-bin
Lowest TR onset temperature at 70% SOH indicates higher TR proneness, not greater safety
QSEI increases substantially with aging while QLi-bin decreases; net effect requires joint evaluation
TR hazard assessment cannot rely on a single heat term in isolation
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming aged cells are definitively more or less hazardous without joint heat term analysis
Generalizing SOH-dependent trends to other cell chemistries or abuse modes
Asserting QLi-bin decrease alone determines lower TR severity in aged cells

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-term TR heat balance across SOH levels; correct interpretation of TR onset temperature as hazard indicator rather than safety indicator.
analysis.primary_boundary: LiFePO4/graphite, 1C overcharge, SOH 55%–100%; post-ISC regime only for heat term analysis.
intended_error_modes: unsupported monotonicity (net hazard decreases with aging based on single term)

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell is charged at 0.5 C. At SOC = 40%, ohmic heat, reversible heat, and reaction heat contribute 47%, 30.7%, and 22.3% of total heat generation respectively. As charging continues beyond SOC = 50% at the same C-rate, which of the following statements are correct?
options: A Reversible heat becomes the dominant heat source, reaching approximately 71.5% of total heat generation at full charge.
B Ohmic heat remains the dominant contributor throughout charging because current density is constant at fixed C-rate.
C The transition in dominant heat source occurs between SOC = 40% and SOC = 50%.
D Reaction heat surpasses ohmic heat and becomes the second-largest contributor at full charge.
E The shift in dominance is driven by the continued rise of reversible heat after SOC = 40%.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Reversible heat proportion reaches 71.5% at full charge, making it dominant at 0.5 C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: At 0.5 C, reversible heat overtakes ohmic heat after SOC=50%; ohmic dominance applies to high C-rate (2.0 C).

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states reversible heat dominates after SOC=50%, placing the transition between SOC=40% and SOC=50%.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports reaction heat surpassing ohmic heat at full charge under 0.5 C conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states reversible heat continues to rise after SOC=40%, driving the dominance shift.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: SOC-dependent heat source dominance at low C-rate and the transition threshold
analysis.primary_boundary: 0.5 C charging; SOC transition between 40% and 50%
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying 0.5 C dominance pattern to high C-rate
Claiming reaction heat becomes second-largest without evidence

solver_view.prompt: During 500 charge-discharge cycles at increasing C-rates, SEI film thickness grows by approximately 70 nm. An engineer proposes that this SEI growth is a significant direct contributor to cell temperature rise during cycling and should be included as a primary heat source in thermal models. Using the evidence about SEI film heat generation and its relationship to C-rate, evaluate whether this proposal is justified and explain the mechanistic reasoning.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The proposal is not justified.
